verses.text: Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers; and they presented themselves before God. Guidance: Joshua picks Shechem deliberately. This is where Abraham first heard 'I will give this land' (Gen 12:6); where Jacob buried foreign gods (Gen 35:4); where Joshua read the law beneath Ebal and Gerizim (Josh 8); and where Joseph's bones will be buried (v32). The most theology-saturated address in the land. The leaders 'present themselves before God' — not before Joshua. This is not a retirement reception; it is a courtroom appearance before the LORD.

Joshua said unto all the people, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel: Your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham, and the father of Nachor: and they served other gods. I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood, and led him throughout all the land of Canaan, and multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac. I gave unto Isaac Jacob and Esau: and I gave unto Esau mount Seir, to possess it; but Jacob and his children went down into Egypt. Guidance: God's first sentence of the covenant renewal is humbling: 'your fathers served other gods.' Abraham was not a believer when God called him — he was a pagan in Ur. The chosen people have idolatrous DNA in their veins. Election does not assume a pristine origin; grace finds people where they are. The Hebrew verb 'I took' (v3) is the same word for picking up a small object — God reaches into pagan Ur and lifts Abraham out.

I sent Moses also and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt, according to that which I did among them: and afterward I brought you out. I brought your fathers out of Egypt: ye came unto the sea; and the Egyptians pursued after your fathers with chariots and horsemen unto the Red sea. When they cried unto the LORD, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians, and brought the sea upon them, and covered them; and your eyes have seen what I have done in Egypt: and ye dwelt in the wilderness a long season. Guidance: 'I sent… I plagued… I brought out.' Eight first-person verbs in three verses. God is the subject of every clause. The exodus is not retold from a human angle — it is God's autobiography. And the listening generation hears 'your eyes have seen.' The miracle did not happen to ancestors only; the children of those who crossed are the ones now standing at Shechem. Memory is not just history — it is identity.

I brought you into the land of the Amorites, which dwelt on the other side Jordan; and they fought with you: and I gave them into your hand, that ye might possess their land; and I destroyed them from before you. Then Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, arose and warred against Israel, and sent and called Balaam son of Beor to curse you: but I would not hearken unto Balaam; therefore he blessed you still: so I delivered you out of his hand. Guidance: The Balaam story (Num 22-24) is told in two lines from heaven's angle. The hired prophet could not get the curse out of his mouth because God overrode the script. Israel did not even know the danger they were in; they were unaware spectators of a battle fought in the air for their souls. The number of unseen rescues in any believer's life is greater than the visible ones.

Ye went over Jordan, and came unto Jericho: and the men of Jericho fought against you, the Amorites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hittites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites; and I delivered them into your hand. I sent the hornet before you, which drave them out from before you, even the two kings of the Amorites; but not with thy sword, nor with thy bow. I have given you a land for which ye did not labour, and cities which ye built not, and ye dwell in them; of the vineyards and oliveyards which ye planted not do ye eat. Guidance: God names seven nations, then states the punchline: 'not with thy sword, nor with thy bow.' Whatever Israel's sword did, God's hornet did more. And then the most quietly humbling verse in the chapter: the land you didn't labor for, cities you didn't build, vineyards you didn't plant, olives you didn't grow. Every breakfast in Canaan is a sermon: you eat what you did not earn. Inheritance is the daily preacher of grace.

Now therefore fear the LORD, and serve him in sincerity and in truth: and put away the gods which your fathers served on the other side of the flood, and in Egypt; and serve ye the LORD. If it seem evil unto you to serve the LORD, choose you this day whom ye will serve; whether the gods which your fathers served that were on the other side of the flood, or the gods of the Amorites, in whose land ye dwell: but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD. Guidance: The chapter's most famous verse. Joshua does not coerce; he offers a true choice — and then publicly takes his own side. 'As for me and my house' — leadership by visible commitment. The verb 'put away' implies Israel still has foreign gods to put away. Even after Jericho, Ai, and the Jordan miracle, there are household idols hiding in the camp. The covenant renewal forces a decision the conquest itself could not.

The people answered: God forbid that we should forsake the LORD, to serve other gods; for the LORD our God, he it is that brought us up and our fathers out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage, and which did those great signs in our sight, and preserved us in all the way wherein we went, and among all the people through whom we passed: and the LORD drave out from before us all the people, even the Amorites which dwelt in the land: therefore will we also serve the LORD; for he is our God. Guidance: The people answer with their own version of God's retelling — quoting back what He just said. They have been catechized well. 'For he is our God' — confession in three words. It will not stop them from failing in Judges, but for this moment, the answer is right. Sometimes faithful liturgy is what gets you through the years between vows and stumbles.

Joshua said unto the people: Ye cannot serve the LORD; for he is an holy God; he is a jealous God; he will not forgive your transgressions nor your sins. If ye forsake the LORD, and serve strange gods, then he will turn and do you hurt, and consume you, after that he hath done you good. The people said unto Joshua: Nay; but we will serve the LORD. Guidance: An astonishing pastoral move. Joshua deliberately discourages them — 'ye cannot serve the LORD.' He wants no light commitment. Cheap vows are worse than no vows. The holiness and jealousy of God are not marketing material; they are the price of entering a real covenant. The people insist; Joshua only accepts after they push back. A serious yes survives a hard 'are you sure?'

Joshua said unto the people: Ye are witnesses against yourselves that ye have chosen you the LORD, to serve him. They said: We are witnesses. Now therefore put away, said he, the strange gods which are among you, and incline your heart unto the LORD God of Israel. The people said unto Joshua: The LORD our God will we serve, and his voice will we obey. Guidance: Joshua makes them officially witnesses against themselves — court language. There will be no excuse later that they didn't know. Then the practical: put away the actual idols. Confession at the meeting is good; clearing the shelf at home is the proof. 'His voice will we obey' — the final commitment is not just allegiance but listening.

So Joshua made a covenant with the people that day, and set them a statute and an ordinance in Shechem. Joshua wrote these words in the book of the law of God, and took a great stone, and set it up there under an oak, that was by the sanctuary of the LORD. Joshua said unto all the people: Behold, this stone shall be a witness unto us; for it hath heard all the words of the LORD which he spake unto us: it shall be therefore a witness unto you, lest ye deny your God. Guidance: A written record (in the book of the law) and a public monument (the great stone under the oak). Double witness — page and rock. The stone has 'heard' — Joshua's metaphor gives ears to creation, much as Habakkuk 2:11 says the stones cry out. When human memory fails, the stone remains. Almost every chapter of Joshua has built a cairn; the book ends with one more. Israel is a people of stacked stones, each one a memo from God to the next generation.

So Joshua let the people depart, every man unto his inheritance. Guidance: The leader's last public act: send them home. Not a parade, not a power-grab, just dispersal. Every man to his own inheritance. The book that began with crossing a river together ends with families separating to till their own fields. Faithful leadership does not gather a permanent crowd; it sends people back to the lives they were given. The covenant is renewed; ordinary life resumes.

After these things, Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died, being an hundred and ten years old. They buried him in the border of his inheritance in Timnath-serah, which is in mount Ephraim, on the north side of the hill of Gaash. Guidance: 'The servant of the LORD' — the same title Moses bore (Deut 34:5). Joshua earns it at the end. 110 years — the same age Joseph reached (Gen 50:26); the Egyptians considered it the ideal lifespan. He is buried in his own little city, Timnath-serah, that he asked for last and built himself. No grand mausoleum. The book's biggest leader gets the book's smallest burial paragraph.

After these things, Joshua son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died, being an hundred and ten years old. They buried him in the border of his inheritance in Timnath-serah, which is in mount Ephraim, on the north side of the hill of Gaash. Guidance: 'The servant of the LORD' — the same title Moses bore (Deut 34:5). Joshua earns it at the end. 110 years — the same age Joseph reached (Gen 50:26); the Egyptians considered it the ideal lifespan. He is buried in his own little city, Timnath-serah, that he asked for last and built himself. No grand mausoleum. The book's biggest leader gets the book's smallest burial paragraph.

Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders that outlived Joshua, and which had known all the works of the LORD, that he had done for Israel. Guidance: A bittersweet line. As long as the eyewitness generation lived, Israel served the LORD. The verse contains the hidden tragedy of the next book: when the eyewitnesses are gone, the people will not. Judges 2:10 explicitly notes 'another generation arose which knew not the LORD.' Faith does not transfer by genetics; it transfers by retelling. The stone in Shechem, the book of the law, the catechism of Joshua 24 — none of it survived the silence that came after. This verse is the book of Joshua's quietest warning.

The bones of Joseph, which the children of Israel brought up out of Egypt, buried they in Shechem, in a parcel of ground which Jacob bought of the sons of Hamor the father of Shechem for an hundred pieces of silver: it became the inheritance of the children of Joseph. Guidance: Joseph's bones — carried from Egypt 400 years before through forty years of wilderness — finally rest. His Gen 50:25 request ('ye shall carry up my bones from hence') has been kept by four generations of faithful pallbearers. Buried in the field Jacob bought (Gen 33:19). The bones lie in a plot legally owned by their family for centuries — a tiny pre-purchased inheritance inside the great inheritance. Faith keeps its old promises. Joseph believed God would bring the family home; he was right, just slowly.

Eleazar the son of Aaron died; and they buried him in a hill that pertained to Phinehas his son, which was given him in mount Ephraim. Guidance: The high priest who served the whole book of Joshua — at the lots, at the altar, alongside Joshua — finally lays down. Buried at his son Phinehas's hill in Ephraim. Three burials close the book: Joshua the leader, Joseph the patriarch, Eleazar the priest. King, prophet, priest types all laid to rest in three verses. The page turns. The book of Joshua ends not with triumphal trumpets but with three quiet graves. The land is given; the leaders are gone; the people remain. The next book will tell what they did when their fathers' grave-markers no longer cast a shadow they could feel.
